meta.topic: Present simple and present continuous for the future

step_1_context.text: We can often use either the present simple or will to talk about future events that are part of some timetabled or programmed arrangement or routine. However, we prefer the present simple for fixed, u

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
Present simple
 We can often use either the present simple or will to talk about future events that are part of some timetabled or programmed arrangement or routine.
step_2_explanation.formula: However, we prefer the present simple for fixed, unchangeable events.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake:
We use the present simple, not will, to refer to the future –
 ☆ in time clauses with conjunctions such as after, as soon as, before, by the time, when, while, until:
 • When you see Ben, tell him he still owes me some money.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 10.1.1 We ___ our exam results on the 20th August.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Not a fixed timetable — use will (uncertain/expected result).

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 10.1.2 Alex ___ our cats while we're away next week.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Personal arrangement, not a programmed fixture — use will.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 10.1.3 I think I'll take an umbrella in case it ___.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: In case + conditional clause → present simple.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 10.1.4 There is a reading list to accompany my lecture, which I ___ at the end.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Not a fixed timetable — use will.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 10.1.5 The new drug ___ on sale in the USA next year.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Not a fixed programme — use will.
